title: Colin Gray found guilty in son's alleged deadly school shooting
author: NBC News
fullText: Breaking news out of Georgia. This has just happened in the last hour or so. The father of an alleged school shooter has been found guilty of seconddegree murder and involuntary manslaughter. Colin Gay's son, Colt Gray, is accused of opening fire at Appalache High School in 2024, killing two students and two teachers and injuring nine others. His father was accused of providing his teenage son access to that firearm used to carry out the shooting. Well, this marks the second school shooting now where a parent has been held criminally responsible for their child's actions. You may remember in 2024, James and Jennifer Crumbley were both found guilty of involuntary manslaughter for a mass shooting committed by their son Ethan at Oxford High School. That was in Michigan. Let's bring in NBC's Aaron Gilchrist. He has been watching this um this verdict unfold. Aaron, tell us more. Well, Savannah, you you note that the fact that this is a parent who's being held responsible for the alleged crimes of his child, and this sort of takes it up another level because in the Crumbly case, we were talking about involuntary manslaughter. In this case, we're talking about seconddegree murder charges. Col Colin Gray was found guilty on two counts of seconddegree murder for the two students who were killed in that school shooting at Appalache High School back in 2024. Uh and this case is sort of unique because there were also charges of cruelty to children. And in order to convict on that seconddegree murder charge, you had to convict on the cruelty to children charge in this case. And really the narrative that prosecutors used as the sort of umbrella narrative in their uh presentation over the course of two weeks and 60 plus witnesses was that because Colin Gray gave his son uh a weapon or gave him access to a weapon that he knew could be used to hurt people and that he knew his son had uh exhibited signs over the years, warning signs if you will, over the years that he could harm someone uh in giving him the weapon. Despite knowing that information, he was guilty of cruelty to children. There were, I think, 18 counts of cruelty to children. Uh to include students, other students who were shot but not killed. To include students who were in the classroom where the shooting happened uh and traumatized by it. Many of them testified in the early days of this trial about the trauma that they experienced, about the injuries that they had. Uh and so all those counts combined together really uh helped the prosecution to to create a picture for this jury that it took them less than two hours to make a decision about. They were able to ask for they the jury actually asked to get the case last night. There were some technical things, some legal things that prevented that from happening. They got it just after 9:00 this morning and uh again in less than two hours the jury came back with a verdict of guilty on all 27 charges that were against this uh this father. And and I think it's also worth pointing out uh in this particular case the prosecutors uh came out afterwards and said that it was really the multit multitude of warnings that this father got that the jury really zeroed in on uh and led them to the decision that they ultimately made today. Savannah >> may be something we continue to see more of as a essentially second of its kind case convicts a parent. Aaron Gilchrist, thank you very much for your reporting here. >> We thank you for watching and remember, stay updated on breaking news and top stories on the NBC News app or watch live on our YouTube channel.
